title: The Pains of Hell and the Glory of Heaven
subtitle: After the Last Judgment: the eternal loss of God, and the joy no tongue can tell

stillness: Let the world's noise fall away.
Set yourself, in silence, before the two ends of all things.
gospel: "nor has it entered into the heart of man"
what God has prepared for those who love him.
And to the others he will say:
behold: Look upon that unending and cruel punishment, that eternal torment; mourn, and weep.
There is the heat of fire and the sharpness of cold, darkness, smoke, inward weeping, the sight of demons.
There is unending outcry, reproach, the hammers of those who strike, serpents and dragons.
There is dryness, thirst, the stench of sulphur, the worm of conscience, chains and prison.
There is fear, grief, shame, and the confusion of sinners laid bare before all.
There is envy, rancor, sorrow, and the taking away of every hope of salvation.
The damned will also see one another: not for any comfort, but that they may be tormented the more.
Behold: there the fire of hell will shine upon the wretched only to increase their misery — that they may see whereby they grieve, never whereby they rejoice.
consider: Because man sinned in his own eternity — that is, for as long as he lived — and his will had no end of sinning, justly he is punished eternally before the eternal God.
Gregory: It belongs to the sentence of the strict Judge that they should never lack torment, whose mind in this life never wished to lack sin.
They who never wished to cease from sin, it is just that they should never be free from punishment.
Above all the pains of hell is the loss of the vision of God; for the wicked grieve not so much over the torment itself as that they are cast out from such fellowship.

Chrysostom: Though you should heap up a thousand hells, you will say nothing equal to being driven from the honor of that blessed glory, to be hateful to Christ, and to hear from him: I never knew you.
To behold that face, full of meekness and tenderness, turning away from us; and those eyes of all calm and sweetness, no longer bearing to look upon us.
Woe to us who do not think upon these evils, but, numb as though secure and neglecting the care of our soul, rush toward them without ceasing!
If each day the damned shed but one material tear, more water would flow from a single damned soul, in the course of time, than all the seas of this world could hold.

Were there a millstone so vast it touched the circuit of heaven, and a tiny bird, coming once every hundred thousand years, plucked from it with its beak but a tenth part of a grain of millet — would that our sentence of eternal damnation might end when at last the whole stone was worn away.
But alas — even this consolation divine justice utterly denies to the wretched.
prayer: But let us by no means suffer this, O only-begotten Son of God,
nor let us undergo that intolerable and dreadful punishment.
Let us not be driven from the honor of that blessed glory,
nor be hateful to you, nor hear from you: I never knew you.
Let us not see that face, full of meekness and tenderness, turned away from us,
nor those eyes of all calm and sweetness refuse to look upon us.
Grant us rather that glory and joy of paradise,
where the joys are so many and so great that no reckoner of this world could number them,
which eye has not seen, nor ear heard, nor has it entered into the heart of man.

latinJewel.latin: Ignis gehennæ lucebit miseris ad miseriæ augmentum, ut videant unde doleant; non ad consolationem, ut videant unde gaudeant.
latinJewel.english: The fire of hell will shine upon the wretched only to increase their misery — that they may see whereby they grieve, not for any comfort, that they might see whereby they should rejoice.
